summary: Victor Zhenyi Wang, a data scientist with a disability, recounts attending a rationalist conference where philosopher Jonathan Anomaly presented on 'liberal eugenics' - voluntary genetic selection for traits like intelligence - to an uncritical audience. Wang challenges the utilitarian premise that disabled lives are 'worse off,' citing disability rights advocates who argue their lives are valuable and that disability-related challenges stem from societal structures, not inherent deficits. He critiques how metrics like QALYs inherit society's biases against disabled people and how cost-benefit analysis has historically been weaponized to deny disabled people basic rights and accommodations. Wang warns that techno-utopian visions built on these frameworks would systematically marginalize disabled people, returning to an era of institutionalization.
key_points: The author experienced the surreal discomfort of being in a room of 'do-gooders' discussing the elimination of people like himself, with no audience pushback
Disabled people argue their lives are not 'worse off' - disability shapes identity, and constraints become the foundation for rich, satisfying lives
Challenges faced by disabled people are properties of society, not inherent to disabilities - like how glasses eliminated shortsightedness as a meaningful limitation
Metrics like QALYs are biased because they rely on non-disabled people's imagined ratings of disabled life quality
Cost-benefit analysis has historically been used to deny disabled people accommodations and basic dignities, from Reagan's executive orders to absurd analyses on preventing prison rape
money_quotes: It is a surreal experience to be in a room full of people who believe that they would like to do good in the world and at the same time be a person who they would prefer not to exist at all.
We take constraints that no one would choose and build rich and satisfying lives within them.
What Johnson is claiming is that it is really a property of society that disabilities constrains the ability for one to attain the capabilities they wish for.
This metric is going to inherit all the biases society already has against disabled people.
The language of cost benefit analysis has become an ubiquitous excuse to deny all sorts of basic rights in American politics.
It is tempting and beyond that terribly easy to use the language of cost effectiveness as an instrument of dispossession.
In that world, which seems not so far off, we will be marked as lesser, our dignities and rights denied to us.
key_insight: The utilitarian logic underlying effective altruism and techno-optimism, when combined with cost-benefit analysis, creates a framework that systematically devalues disabled lives and has historically been used to deny them basic rights and dignity.
